speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 3 of an interview with Rachel Kessler on July 29th, 1996. Mrs. Kessler, how long was your journey over to America? How long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two months on the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on two boats. How long the first one was, don't know. To Martinique, about-- about a month, I guess. I guess about a month, four or five weeks, we were on the way to Martinique. It wasn't a quick boat, and it was a long way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then another four or five weeks on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: --second boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- we were in the Martinique for a while. And the-- the-- the last boat didn't take more than a week or 10 days. It wasn't-- from the Martinique to New York is not that far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a little bit more the conditions on board the

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think there's anything to describe. There were no-- they-- they didn't pamper us. That's all. And they-- otherwise we were together, and we knew we were going to arrive safely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it very crowded?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a bigger boat, I think. It was a bigger boat. The first one had been a cattle boat without any cattle. But there was room for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: May-- I think it was May the 30th, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your first impressions? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that point you don't have any impressions. See, it was a very-- it was a very [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] strong thing to be meeting with my sister whom I had not seen since I was little.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that that you met her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you arrive and meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was waiting for us in New York when we arrived, and then we drove off to her house. And then Martin looked for work, and he immediately found work because he was very special-- specialist. And so we left, and we went to live-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have difficulty remembering names-- upstate New York in a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was it like to try to live and work in America at first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some things were strange. Shopping was very strange. To come from what we had-- where we had come from when there was so little food to be had, in Marseille especially, and to see the supermarkets here with piles and heaps, rows in high-- of-- of different kinds of fruit. It was a-- I've never forgotten it. It was quite a shock to see how people could eat in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: You then had a son in America. When was that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack Alexander.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to be in contact with your family in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you learn of your father's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about a-- a year and a half after we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which would've been?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the middle of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the time when your-- your husband found out about his mother and sisters, what that was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't remember how we found out, because we had received something from them that I have. I'm sure I wouldn't throw a thing away like that, but I've looked desperately. I have not been able to locate it among all the papers. We heard from them, because we would inform them. When we arrived in this country, we'd inform them we were here. And they sent either a letter or a telegram or something that they were so happy to feel that we were safe. But none of us knew, at that time, that they were not safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my three brothers were all soldiers in World War I. One of them was also in World War II. And so nothing of interest in that direction.

speaker: interviewer
text: They all stayed in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they all stayed in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any antisemitism in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not directly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was rather surprised when there was a couple in-- Batavia was the name, the first job Martin had. And a couple came very kindly to visit us, and the woman asked me brightly what was my maiden name. And I looked at her, and I knew what she-- she wanted to see if it was a Jewish name. She wanted to find out if we were Jewish. And my thought was right away, this is a funny country. This is a funny country, I said to myself. I mean, I told her Saunders, she said, oh. She seemed quite relieved. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You wrote a letter to Eleanor Roosevelt. What-- what was that all about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. Martin did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that all about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were pretty desperate. Because Martin had this job, everything's fine, we think. And then Pearl Harbor happened, and the American government gave orders that any non-citizens in firms making munitions of any kind were to be suspended, to be sent off away. So Martin-- we were not. We put in for citizenship right away. We-- in five years, we became citizens, approximately. There were some old German workmen in that who'd been there for years and years but never bothered to get American citizenship. So they were laid off, and Martin was laid off. And then they were allowed back on when the whole thing was arranged, and they didn't take Martin back on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- we didn't have a job. And he began looking for a job and had difficulty finding one. Then he found one-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where was it-- in the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the company-- well, he found a job. But they couldn't engage him because he would have to have a special permit because of being a foreigner, and he tried everything he could.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he wrote to Eleanor Roosevelt, and then right away he had an appointment in Washington. And he went to Washington, and I think it was a colonel-- he may remember the name-- who received him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was supposed to-- the idea was he was to get a permit, because he'd found a job. And if he got the permit, he could have the job. And this man said, why don't you Jews take your family somewhere else? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But eventually then he-- he went to another man and-- who knew how to handle it, and he got the job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Oneida Limited, that's the name of the company, Oneida. And actually, the factory of Oneida is in a village called Sherrill, and Oneida is a mile or so away. And we got friendly with the Jews in Oneida. There were about maybe a dozen families. And Martin found that they had no-- no congregation of any kind. So being a rabbi, basically, he arranged for having Friday night services at one of the homes. Well, I guess the people around must have found out that he was a Jew, and the whole workforce of Oneida went on strike. Now, that had happened also in Paris, in a job he had in Paris, where he was to be-- because he was-- he knew-- he was clever, and they-- they were going to advance him a little bit or something. And the whole workforce of the factory went on strike. So that happened to him both in France and in this country. And the-- the manager in Oneida said he was very, very sorry. There was nothing he could do about it, but he would recommend him somewhere else. So he recommended him somewhere else, and he had this job in Kearny, New Jersey, and they were working for the Navy, doing munitions for the Navy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was together with another engineer, and what they wanted them to do was to design implements that could be used after the war when-- when they no longer had to make munitions. But they would still need to manufacture. They had a big workforce there with workmen and so on. So I've come across in the papers something he worked out very, very beautifully in great-- great detail. But the day the war was over, that place closed down. It was only there in-- for what it was there for. That was all. Just closed down. So again without a job. And he had a heart attack. Martin had got a heart attack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he-- well, he better get a rabbinic job. So he got a rabbinic job fairly easily, and from then on, let me see, he had one, two, three. And he enjoyed from the point of view that he came into contact with other rabbis. He joined the Rabbinical Assembly. He came to meetings of the Jewish Theological Seminary, and that was fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: However, there wasn't-- really not much future in it. So after three years in Oak Ridge-- and at that time Eisenhower was the president, and-- what was it here? He was throwing open the use of-- of electricity-- not electricity, explosive, whatever it was, for regular manufacturing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a number of the scientists-- because those were practically all-- practically all the members of his congregation were in that kind of work in Oak Ridge. We lost a lot of membership, and he decided, well, he would look for a scientific job. So he found one very easily up here, up north, and we came up north.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when-- when was that that you came up north?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1957. And so-- but he still wanted his rabbinic work. So he was rabbi-- a weekend rabbi, the-- a number-- a number of small congregations that were growing around-- around Boston. I imagine it's similar in-- in a lot of big towns. There'd be a few-- a few Jewish families in a-- in a suburb, and they would want their children to learn, and they would start a school. They would take a rabbi for the weekends, and then eventually they would manage to be able to build and-- and to go further and take a full-time rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was full-time rabbi in several, one after the other. The last one was in-- in-- it was in-- in-- Revere was the last one. Before, he was in Salem once. And in Salem, he was there for one year. And then they decided-- [INAUDIBLE] then they decided they could afford to have a-- a rabbi full time, which they have now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they liked him very much, and they begged him to stay. He-- they just begged him to stay. But he refused, because he was established now in a regular job where-- thank goodness he did, because now we have a pension to live on. [LAUGHS] And the last one-- the last one was Revere, the last one. But by that time, he was in his 80s.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like to move around so much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. We moved about 10 times. I've never calculated exactly. And several times he had to leave before I could leave, and I was left with the removal to-- oh, I don't know what to say. What can you say? It's something to do, and you have to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you accompany your husband when he went to Israel in the '60s?

speaker: interviewee
text: '60s. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He received a medal.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went on a trip to Israel after the 1967 War. Well, that was the trip. Then in 1970-- 1969, I don't know how the-- the Israelis found out about his-- his knowledge and skill, because he was doing quite important metallurgical work, developing the titanium metals and so on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Who got in touch with him? He knows, but I forget now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- they-- they want him to come for three months. They had some metallurgical problems, and so could he come? So he-- he didn't say-- he said-- they said they wanted him to lecture there. The-- the-- it was called a lecture tour. So he got leave of absence for three months to do a lecture tour in Israel, and he went in the beginning of December. And I was working at Hanscom Field, and I gave notice and left in January and joined him then. So he was there three months, and I was there two-- two months with him. Did you ever go back to Nottingham?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back in 1984. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, my-- I only have one sister living there. I have a number of cousins, and she got in touch with them and there was a meeting of the clan. So that was a strange meeting. I'd known them as-- they were little children. In fact, some of them were ones who'd come over from Lodz as-- as-- as tiny children and lived in our house until their father could get established. So that was quite a meeting, too, 1984.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you then become active in the Jewish community in America at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, I was always active in some way. When he was rabbi, I would help in the teaching. I'd do some of the teaching, and I would organize things. When we were here, I had a Young Judaea group in-- in-- in the '40s. No, not the '40s. We weren't here in the '40s, were we? [LAUGHS] But when we came here-- no, we came here in '57-- in the '60s I had a Young Judaea group for a few years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I was very much involved from 19-- from the winter of 1970 approximately until about 1990 with a senior Jew-- Jewish group. It was not affiliated with any one synagogue. There was a certain loose help by CJP who gave us a bus for a while. We had members from all the surrounding towns here-- Belmont, Waltham, Woburn, Lexington, and so on, and we had a pretty strong group.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the Jewish Community Center in Newton was established, and they wanted us to join there. They wanted to get everybody to join there. Well, it's a-- quite a long way from here, even though we're considered west, I suppose. It's quite a long way. And what could they do for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they invited us once to look around and they said, well, we'll send a bus. Well, one bus wouldn't do because we're scattered. Well, we'll arrange something. They really were very eager to have us. So I was president by that time and had been for a while. And we voted on it, and they voted that they wanted to stay independent. So we stayed independent, and it petered out eventually. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What else shall I say? A couple of weeks ago, I turned over the papers-- I had a big backlog of information on this group, which I turned over to the Jewish Historical Society. Had-- young lady came out from-- from Waltham, and we turned all that stuff over to her. When did your husband start doing the woodworking?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 1970-- I can't remember, 1972, '73.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the woodworking a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: He really would have liked to work in metal, because that's-- that's what he [INAUDIBLE]. Basically, he was a very good mechanical engineer because he'd learned with his father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But anything to do with metal cost just too much money. There was no-- no question. And he began to-- began to do it. He just began and did it. I mean, he's a miracle. I mean, the-- that curly one down-- his [INAUDIBLE] Curls he used to call-- standing there, I think that's the first big one he made. But then he continued and made and made and just beautiful work. Some of it religious, some of it not. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he became-- it became fairly well known.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he's-- I mean, if he'd started when he was younger and got an agent or some kind of organization-- because we're not businessmen, either of us. We're not clever with money. And he-- he was never doing anything where he could accumulate anything. We're just lucky that we both worked for the government, and we have pensions. [LAUGHS] But his work is just so absolutely marvelous, so beautiful, and he worked it and smoothed it. And I don't know how he did it. I just don't know how.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last couple of questions. How do you think-- do you think that your experience in Europe affected the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think because I grew up in an English Jewish home, that would be what would continue for me. It would-- I-- I sent my little girl to a Hebrew, after school, little school when she was old enough. And I kept kosher. Now, I must say it was not that easy keeping kosher in Paris. And the first few years that I lived there, after my work I used to go in the evening to the other end of Paris, taking the subway, to buy my kosher meat and then bring it home and-- and kosher it, kosher it because it was not kosher the way we get it here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to soak it and so on [INAUDIBLE], and then Friday, to-- after my-- after my working, to come home and cook-- cook it. So-- but I did it, because you had something you had to do and you did it, right? Is there a message that you would leave for future generations based on your own experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe from the Jewish point of view, being involved in the community. Now, recently, something came up about the Dalai Lama. Now, the Dalai Lama is very interested in finding out how come Judaism has-- has-- has been kept up, how the Jewish people has kept up. There was a book came out about a year or so ago that I read-- forgotten the title or the author, but I'm sure it's to be found-- describing a meeting in India between several Jewish leaders and the Dalai Lama, where they questioned one another and talked together. They did Shabbos together and so on. And they had-- there were some of his monks there, and they did a lot of talking and discussing. He's a charming personality. He's just a wonderful personality. And so someone, I don't know where, whether it was there or later, that came with the idea that Jewishness persisted because of the family, that it was the family that kept it. I feel that the community is very important. We are lucky to be in a-- a community where there's a-- an empathetic rabbi and a number-- quite a number of the hoi polloi, the-- the-- the members, who are interested and will-- will take jobs and help and be in committees and do things. And a good school with a good leader and a good preschool and all with something going on. They have established a-- a-- a 55 Plus, which kind of replaces what I was doing. But I-- and I did try myself when I saw that my group was dwindling to the point where-- so I went to-- I got-- I called the Sisterhood president, let me come and talk. I talked to this Sisterhood, to that Sisterhood, the other Sisterhood. I talked to the rabbis. I-- I-- and what I was trying to do was to get people to join. But I'm not an organizer from the business kind of point of view. I'm not good at it, and I didn't really know the way to go about it. I got it started here. But the big mistake was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the rabbi said, speak to the president. The president said speak to so-and-so. He'll organize something for you. And I got it organized, and I said to this man-- now we-- we arranged a meeting. And I said, whatever you do, don't mention my name. This is nothing to do with the Jewish leisure group. It's nothing to do with it. Remember, that's the most important thing. Don't you dare mention my name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to the meeting. And I persuaded some of my group to come too, because I wanted them also to feel they could join this group. But they were old people, you know? They were elderly like me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This man got up, and they began the meeting, and he didn't know what to say. He had not prepared himself at all apparently. And he's-- looked around helplessly, then he said, well, I'll ask Rachel Kessler to explain. Well, that was the end of it. It didn't work, absolutely. There were 50 meeting-- 50 people at that meeting. Next time, there were 25 people. Next time, there were 12. And I said, [INAUDIBLE] let's forget it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then somebody else has started a 55 group, which is very, very successful. I mean, people at 55, that's a good idea. They don't think-- they don't think they're old at 55. But to see me sitting at a table with several gray-haired people, that was too much for them. They-- they didn't want to be with people like that. But you would say be active in the community too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say so. Not now, I'm too busy with my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mrs. Kessler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I thank you too. I think you've been very nice and handled things very nicely. Did he go to bed? Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us about this photograph? Who-- who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alexander Levinsky, yes, Levi Saunders, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- he was a tailor. Because he wasn't in a situation to earn a living. He was born in Poland and went to live in-- in-- in England. And he went to night school to learn English and caught in with somebody who taught him how to be a tailor, and that's what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know. I have no idea. I never thought about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any idea of how old--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he's- he's a middle-aged man there. That was-- it's certainly in England. It was probably-- probably in Nottingham, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mrs. Kessler, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother, Emma Levinsky. She had eight children--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --all healthy. I don't know what else you'd like me to say. She was a good cook, and she enjoyed her own cooking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been taken in Nottingham. That's all I can say. Because she's-- that was what year, somebody said? Taken in Nottingham, must have been.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was 15, my rabbi, who was Rabbi Kayensky in Nottingham, had me study to take an examination at Jews' College, London. Usually this was an examination taken by young men, maybe 19 or 20 years old. So this was quite unusual, because I was only 15 at the time-- this was-- 16. The certificate makes it 16. That's all right. And it was several days where I had to go and sit in the hazzan's house to answer papers and translate Hebrew, translate an unseen out of a-- out of the rest. I had to know Chumash well. The first five books I had to know well. Then there was going to be one that I had not been told beforehand, and I-- I was very, very angry when I did it because it was so easy. It turned out to be the book of-- oh, never mind. [LAUGHS] It was something that I knew the story so well that it was too easy to translate, and I really felt very angry about it. Are you interested in that little bit about the-- about the Ten Commandments and one of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we'll-- because we got that already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?
